rows.context_description: Term names divine indignation as sustained moral disposition — the ongoing inner displeasure of God toward wickedness, felt daily
Term names human indignation as responsive inner emotion — the emotional-moral reaction of moral displeasure arising from provocation or perceived injustice
Term names what God finds morally detestable in human inner character and valuation — the hidden life assessed by divine moral perception
Term names the abominable inner character produced by the gap between professed knowledge of God and actual conduct
Term names the inner disposition of revulsion toward evil — what a person claims as morally repugnant, whether genuinely or hypocritically held
Term names the person whose inner moral character is itself the object of divine revulsion — character as abhorrent in God's sight
Term names the fearsome quality that breeds inner self-deception — pride of heart that trusts in its own terrible reputation rather than God
Term names the ritual category of defilement as a boundary of inner spiritual integrity — what the inwardly devoted person refuses as incompatible with devotion to God
Term names the inner orientation of heart, soul, and eyes toward detestable objects — gaze, desire, and delight directed at idolatrous things
Term names the inner character conformation that results from attachment to detestable things — the person becoming like what they love
Term names the commanded inner revulsion — the active rejection of what is unclean as an expression of obedience and moral self-preservation
Term names the reflexive self-defilement — the inner corruption that results from transgression, making the person themselves detestable
Term names divine non-revulsion toward the afflicted — God does not abhor those who suffer; affliction does not make a person detestable to God
Term names the objects of idolatrous inner devotion — the detestable things toward which misdirected worship is oriented
Term names divine inner loathing directed specifically at pride — God abhors the arrogance of Jacob
Term names divine moral assessment of inner character as detestable — the wicked person's thoughts, heart, and dispositions as abomination before God
Term names worship as abominable when it arises from a corrupted inner moral condition — the sacrifices of the wicked are detestable
Term names the failure of inner shame — the conscience that can no longer feel appropriate disgrace over its own abomination
Term names inner grief and self-loathing as response to abomination — the inner movement of sorrow over one's own detestable conduct as the beginning of restoration
Term names the categories of conduct that God finds abominable — violations of holiness that offend divine character and defile the person
Term names the deep inner disposition of abhorrence toward falsehood and evil — the morally alert person's visceral revulsion paired with love of what is true
Term names the inverted disposition of abhorrence — the capacity for moral revulsion directed against justice and truth rather than evil
Term names divine sustained inner abhorrence toward those who have persistently corrupted themselves or violated covenant
Term names the covenantal act whose content is the writing of divine law on the inner mind and heart — inner transformation as the substance of the new covenant
Term names purposive inner intention — the act of setting a planned goal before the will, whether human or divine
Term names the odium-quality of inner moral failure — the stench-metaphor expressing how wickedness and folly produce corruption visible to others
Term names directed inner volitional striving toward God and spiritual goals — the will engaged in sustained effort through all available means
Term names the sustained inner volitional effort of the faith-life — contending, running, fighting as the core experience of spiritual struggle
Term names inner volitional resistance directed against sin — the striving of the will against a named inner adversary
Term names the inner volitional contending for the faith — earnest defence of what has been entrusted to the inner person
Term names the inner perseverance that overcomes in faith — the volitional struggle that achieves its goal
Term names the inner volitional striving alongside another in prayer — shared struggle as intercession
Term names the inner adornment of honour — filigree settings as a metaphor for the precious quality with which the person of character is clothed
Term names the severing of inner bonds of unity — the cutting of fraternal ties that once held community together
Term names the shattering of the inner person through affliction — spirit broken as devastation, the inner life experienced as destroyed
Term names the destructive inner character that plans wickedness — counsel as the instrument of inner malice and harmful scheming
Term names the inner consequence of disregarding divine warning — the ruin that follows upon treating the word of God with contempt
Term names the moral-economic violation in pledge-taking — inner conduct that exploits the vulnerable through use of what is essential to life
Term names the inner orientation toward wisdom — the person who seeks and receives counsel as an expression of inner humility and aspiration
Term names counsel as the expression of inner character — the quality of a person's counsel reveals the quality of their inner life
Term names counsel as the instrument of inner planning — the deliberate use of guidance in forming decisions that achieve their purpose
Term names the inner authority that directs spiritual engagement — the commanding voice that calls the person to prayer in extremity
Term names the pledge as a diagnostic of inner moral character — pledge-handling as the outer expression of inner righteous or wicked orientation
Term names the pledge as a diagnostic of inner moral character — pledge-handling as the outer expression of inner righteous or wicked orientation
Term names the reckless person's inner dissociation from consequence — the pilot/helmsman metaphor applied to the one who lies at the heart of the sea in unguarded folly
Term names the anguish of anticipatory suffering — inner distress experienced in the face of what is coming
Term names the righteous soul's inner suffering from surrounding wickedness — torment produced by immersion in a morally corrupted environment
Term names the anticipated inner dread of divine torment — the fear-state produced by the prospect of deserved judgment
Term names unceasing inner-experiential torment — the absolute absence of rest as the defining quality of final suffering
Term names torment as the object of inner dread — suffering as counterpart to pride and as the judgement that answers self-exaltation
Term names the fully conscious inner experience of torment beyond death — suffering in which the person sees, knows, remembers, and grieves
Term names disease as a condition in which the inner person encounters divine restoration — illness as the context of forgiveness and healing of the whole person
Term names the birth-moment of inner emergence — the person breaking forth from constraint into new life or new capacity
Term names the inner birth-pangs of spiritual formation — the anguish of bringing another person to the fullness of Christ
Term names the inner participation in creation's anguish — the corporate groan of all things awaiting redemption, shared inwardly by those who hope
Term names hospitality as a leadership character requirement — the inner disposition of welcome as essential qualification for those who shepherd others
Term names hospitality as requiring inner willingness without grumbling — the outer act of welcome only genuine when accompanied by the inner disposition of joy
Term names the practice of love for strangers as an inner disposition expressed in conduct — hospitality as a concrete expression of inner orientation toward others
Term names the inner disposition of kindness toward human persons — benevolent orientation toward others as a consistent inner quality
Term names the inner disposition of self-love as a character disqualifier — the person centred on themselves rather than God and others as foundational corruption
Term names the inner disposition of kindly consideration toward others — the orientation of courteous goodwill that governs relational conduct
Term names self-seeking rivalry as an inner disposition — the heart oriented toward self-promotion, expressed through competitive motivation and producing disorder
Term names the positive inner stimulation of motivation — one person's zeal provoking another to greater inner response and generosity
Term names the negative inner provocation that crushes the spirit — conduct by an authority figure that damages the inner person and produces discouragement
Term names inner aspiration toward pleasing God — the will directed entirely toward divine approval as the supreme ambition of the person
Term names inner aspiration toward a quality of personal life — the ambitious pursuit of quietness, dignity, and diligent self-governance
Term names divine indignation as purposive instrument of judgment — God's inner wrath deployed purposively against wickedness and idolatry
Term names the prophet's inner state as divinely imparted indignation — God's own indignation placed within the person called to speak for him
Term names rage as inner fury producing visible physical transformation and extreme action — the Aramaic term for anger at its most intense somatic expression
Term names divine wrath as consuming inner fire — the heat of God's anger poured out in judgment, spending itself completely
Term names anger as the inner state that transforms countenance and drives extreme action — human fury at its most visible and disruptive
Term names the inner heat of habitual anger — the fury characteristic as a settled disposition that requires management and produces destruction when uncontrolled
Term names the inner warming process of developing intensity — anger or passionate feeling as a thermal process building from within through meditation
Term names the kindling of inner anger as the first act of moral rebellion — Cain's anger at divine rejection as the origin-point of the anger characteristic in Scripture
Term names divine anger as the inner response to Israel's repeated covenant violation — the burning of God's wrath in the moment of particular transgression
Term names the inner injunction against anger as spiritual counsel — do not let anger kindle; cease from it; it leads only to harm
Term names the burning intensity of divine wrath at its maximum — fierce anger as the pinnacle of God's response to covenant violation, carrying cosmic consequences
Term names human burning anger as the inner response to perceived injustice or affront — the fierce heat of personal wrath that drives consequential action
Term names divine relational anger — God's inner anger toward specific persons in response to covenant violation, expressed personally and resolved personally
Term names quick-temperedness as a disqualifying character trait — habitual anger as evidence of inner instability incompatible with leadership
Term names the anger that hardens into danger without resolution — the sunset-bounded inner state that must be addressed before becoming settled resentment
Term names the excellent spirit as the seat of extraordinary inner character — wisdom, understanding, and moral quality concentrated in the inner spirit as divine endowment
Term names the hardened spirit as the inner seat of pride — the spirit that stiffens against God, producing arrogant conduct and consequent judgment
Term names the anxious spirit as the inner reception of divine vision — the spirit overwhelmed and troubled by apocalyptic revelation
Term names inner fury driving political and power conflicts — fierce anger in an authority figure expressed through hostile action
Term names the provoking of divine vexation — Israel's conduct as the trigger of God's inner anger, creating a recurring covenantal pattern of provocation and response
Term names the inner vexation of relational cruelty — the distress produced in a person's inner life by another's deliberately provocative conduct
Term names the inner lodging of vexation as a marker of folly — anger that settles and remains as a characteristic of the unwise person
Term names the vexation of inner grief and provocation — the broad register of inner distress from existential burden, rivalry, and sorrow expressed before God in prayer
Term names the anger that lodges in the fool's heart — inner vexation as the resident condition of one who lacks inner governance
Term names the conduct that provokes divine inner anger — Israel's actions causing God's vexation, with covenantal consequences
Term names fierce inner anger co-existing with grief and loyalty — the intensity qualifier that characterises anger at its most burning while accompanying care
Term names the trembling restraint of anger — being agitated without sin, holding inner fury still before God in the privacy of the heart
Term names the somatic terror produced by divine power — the body overwhelmed by the experience of God's glory and judgment
Term names grief-disturbance — the agitation of inner pain expressing itself as trembling and mourning in response to devastating loss
Term names the provocation of divine agitation — conduct that disturbs God's inner relational rest, stirring him to respond
Term names the sharp inner conflict of disagreement — the cutting edge of contention between persons of conviction
Term names the deliberate inner-stimulation toward love and good works — the intentional provoking of others' inner life toward what is good
Term names blasphemous contempt as the inner disposition of rebellion — the speech-act of reviling that reveals the heart's rejection of what is holy
Term names disgrace as an inner condition in extremity — the shame-experience accompanying crisis that overwhelms all capacity for response
Term names the trembling heart as divinely imposed inner instability — inner anxiety and restlessness given by God as a consequence of covenantal displacement
Term names the prophetically embodied inner state of trembling anxiety — the prophet enacting the inner condition of those under judgment as a sign
Term names contempt as the inner disposition of treating what is sacred as beneath regard — the spurning of God, his word, and his servants
Term names contempt directed at reproof — the inner rejection of correction that closes the person to growth and repentance
Term names the inner speech of contempt — what the wicked person says in his heart as the location of his dismissal of God
Term names the inner bearing of grudge — sustained anger retained in the heart against another, named as the interior counterpart to the outer act of vengeance
Term names the duration of divine anger — how long God holds wrath and whether it resolves into mercy, with the answer consistently favouring mercy
Term names the fall of the face as the first bodily expression of inner anger — countenance as the mirror of the inner emotional state
Term names verbal aggression as the outer expression of inner hostility — railing and abuse as manifestations of the contemptuous inner character
Term names divine anger as the inner response to persistent covenant violation — God's wrath kindled by Israel's rebellion against his ways
Term names human anger as the inner response to subordinate failure or betrayal — the superior's wrath provoked by those under his authority
Term names divine anger as momentary against the backdrop of everlasting love — the transient quality of God's wrath contrasted with the permanence of his compassion
Term names the communal consequences of anger — how one person's anger destabilises the whole community around them
Term names the act of provoking divine inner anger — the causal triggering of God's wrath through covenant violation, with historical consequence
Term names inner fury triggered by perceived betrayal — the rage that erupts when deception is discovered, driving violent disproportionate response
Term names the inner capacity for endurance under increasing pressure — the ability of the inner person to sustain more difficult circumstances without collapsing
Term names competition as an exposure of inner character — striving for display reveals the poverty of the inner life compared with the practice of justice
Term names divine wrath as the revealed inner response to human unrighteousness and ungodliness — anger as the consistent inner orientation of God toward sin
Term names human anger as requiring governance — the inner state that must be put away, not nursed, as incompatible with righteous living
Term names righteous anger co-existing with grief — the simultaneous presence of anger and sorrow as the inner response to hardness of heart
Term names morally accountable anger — the inner state of anger that exists under divine command and requires governance before it becomes sin
Term names anger as an inner action-driver — the fury that compels the person to respond, act, or enforce consequences
Term names anger as cosmic inner rebellion against God — the hostility of nations and powers in ultimate opposition to divine rule
Term names righteous inner spirit-provocation — the spirit of the person stirred to righteous indignation by idolatry and the honour of God
Term names love's non-irritability — the inner disposition of love that displaces reactive anger and refuses to be provoked
Term names anger as a provoked inner response to injustice or disobedience — the arousal of wrath in another as consequence of conduct
Term names divine anger as the response to idolatry — the kindling of God's wrath at Israel's most direct covenant violation
Term names divine anger as tempered by steadfast love — the characterisation of God whose anger is slow and bounded by covenant faithfulness
Term names human anger as the inner response to relational conflict — the kindling of personal wrath in the face of provocation, rejection, or perceived injustice
Term names the mastery of anger as the measure of inner greatness — governing anger as exceeding military conquest in demonstration of inner strength
Term names divine anger as the experience of hiddenness — the face turned away as the form of wrath, producing inner desolation
Term names the inner heat of anger as a smoldering thermal process — the self-sustaining build of inner fury toward eruption, using the oven-and-fire metaphor
Term names the anguish of supreme inner crisis driving intensified prayer and somatic manifestation
Term names the overwhelming pangs of extreme distress — anguish described through the labour-birth metaphor: seized, overtaking, coming upon
Term names sorrow as a sustained inner state — grief dwelling in the heart, consuming life, carried as constant companion
Term names sorrow as something overcome and reversed by God — grief that gives way to gladness in divine restoration
Term names the constraining quality of anguish and its resolution — inner experience of being hemmed in, cramped, and the divine deliverance to spaciousness
Term names the extreme distress of covenantal curse — the crushing inner and circumstantial compression of siege and covenant abandonment
Term names personal inner distress met with delight in God's commandments — anguish that does not extinguish inner faith
Term names the narrow straits of extreme inner distress — the pressing constraint from which prayer rises and God responds with spaciousness
Term names the bitterness of soul in deep distress — the inner taste-quality of grief, anguish, and sorrow experienced inwardly
Term names the bitter intensity of anger — the fierce, biting quality of wrath in action and in character
Term names the bitterness of the human condition — the taste of life as toil, futility, and moral poison under the sun
Term names the groaning that arises from inner tumult — the body's expression of the heart's distress breaking out as sound
Term names the toil and misery of human labour as inner experience — the wearing quality of work that produces no satisfaction, the burden of striving under the sun
Term names trouble and misery as an inner condition brought about by sin, oppression, or the human condition
Term names the mischief and harm done by the wicked — the trouble they produce in others as expression of inner malice
Term names the anguish of concern for another in spiritual peril — the inner distress of love that cannot rest when those it cares for are at risk
Term names the narrow distress from which the person cries to God and receives divine response with spaciousness
Term names distress as the divinely appointed condition that produces return to God — anguish as the instrument of covenantal restoration
Term names the affliction overcome in Christ — anguish as the condition the world inflicts but Christ has conquered, giving inner peace amid tribulation
Term names affliction as the ground of inner character formation — anguish producing endurance, character, and hope through divine working
Term names the anguish of shared suffering and consolation — the inner participation in Christ's afflictions as the ground of mutual comfort
Term names the anguish of the great tribulation — the maximum suffering of the end times as the context of final purification and divine salvation
Term names the anguish of being given over to enemies — distress as the covenantal consequence of rebellion, with deliverance as its resolution
Term names anguish as a reliable divine refuge — the God who is found present precisely in times of distress
Term names the distress of the righteous as the condition from which God delivers — anguish as the ground of divine rescue
Term names the anguish of crisis as the place of prayer and divine hearing — distress driving the person to call upon God with the expectation of response
Term names sudden dread as the inner experience of divine judgment — the onset of panic and desolation when God's wrath arrives
Term names the shortness of spirit that closes the inner ear — the anguish of impossible burden that renders the person unable to receive even the word of promise
Term names evil as the circumstantial expression of anguish — the distress and calamity that befalls the person as the condition from which God hides and delivers
Term names the inner recognition of one's own evil conduct — the naming of wrongdoing by the person who has done it as a moral self-assessment
Term names anguish seizing the person in the moment of mortal extremity — the sudden inner grip of distress in the face of death
Term names the anguish of post-mortem torment — intense inner suffering in the afterlife state, in full consciousness of memory and relationship
Term names the anguish of deep relational distress — inner suffering produced by anticipated permanent separation from those deeply loved
Term names unceasing anguish of heart over the spiritual condition of others — sustained inner suffering grounded in love and grief
Term names inner pangs produced by disordered desire — the self-inflicted suffering of those who love money, piercing themselves with their own craving
Term names inner anguish under eschatological judgment — intense inner-physical suffering that drives hardening rather than repentance
Term names the eschatological abolition of inner pain — the promised end of all anguish in the renewed creation
Term names distress as the condition from which people cry to God — inner compression that produces prayer and from which God delivers
Term names the anguish of eschatological judgment — the inner terror of the day of distress
Term names the divine rousing of the human spirit to action — God stirring the inner person to a specific volitional response
Term names the restraint of inner love-arousal — the guarding of love against premature or disordered awakening
Term names the inner moral stirring of the person toward righteousness or God — self-rousing of the inner life in response to moral wrong or spiritual need
Term names the groaning of inner distress and suppressed grief — somatic expression of inner suffering breaking through in sound
Term names the anguish of the shortcut — the inner impatience that drives the spirit to abandon the divinely appointed path
Term names the divine yielding to inner distress — God himself moved by the misery of his people
Term names the short-tempered person as one lacking inner governance — the outer pattern of quick anger as the expression of inner deficiency
Term names the anguish of heart that accompanies deep pastoral care — inner compression of spirit expressed in tears and written in love
Term names the collective anguish of peoples at cosmic upheaval — inner perplexity and distress as the familiar order dissolves
Term names the inner refreshment of the satisfied soul — the prosperity and fat-richness of the inner person whose desire is fulfilled
Term names the anointing that signals divine presence and favour — the saturation of the head with oil as the emblem of divine blessing upon the inner person
Term names the anointing of inner perception — the invitation to receive what opens the eyes of the heart to see one's true spiritual condition
Term names the anointed guardian cherub as the one whose privileged inner access to God was corrupted by pride — anointing as the mark of exceptional inner standing before God
Term names the anointing that confers the Spirit — the act of anointing as the occasion of the Spirit rushing upon the person, transforming the inner life
Term names the sacred inviolability of the anointed — the inner prohibition against harming the one whom God has set apart through anointing
Term names anointing as divine appointment and blessing — the act that designates the person for a calling and marks divine favour upon them
Term names the anointing oil as the sacred medium of consecration — the oil prepared and applied in the act of setting apart persons and objects for divine service
Term names the anointing oil as the sacred medium of consecration — the oil prepared and applied in the act of setting apart persons and objects for divine service
Term names the anointed priest whose inner condition carries covenantal weight — the priest whose sin or purity affects the whole community
Term names the anointed king as the person set apart for divine rule — the one whom God has chosen and whose protection is sacred
Term names the anointed one whose coming is the fulfilment of divine promise — Messiah as the eschatological figure whose appearance marks the consummation of God's purpose
Term names the deliberate abstention from anointing as a practice of mourning — not anointing as the outward expression of inner grief before God
Term names the anointing of restoration — God anointing the person as the act of claiming and beautifying what had been degraded
Term names the anointing of the eyes as the restoration of inner perception — the outer act whose purpose is the return of the capacity to see
Oil as metaphor for quality and character of the inner person
Oil of gladness — inner joy and divine bestowing of flourishing
Oil as outward sign of divine appointment and inner consecration
Oil as marker of disordered inner orientation — self-indulgence and spiritual unfaithfulness
Oil as image of inner penetration — reaching the deepest interior of the person
Term names anointing in anticipation of death — the act of love that prepares the body of Christ for burial, performed in advance through devotion
Term names the antichrist as the paradigm of inner spiritual deception — the spirit of denial as the inner orientation that refuses to acknowledge Christ
Term names the making fat of the inner heart as the judgment that removes perception — God causing the heart to become dull and unresponsive to his voice
Term names the spiritual fatness of prosperity that turns the heart from God — abundance producing inner dullness and self-sufficiency in place of dependence
Term names the blotting out of sin as the erasure of the inner record — anointing-reversal as the act of forgiveness that wipes away what stands against the person
Term names the eschatological wiping away of tears and sorrow — the final reversal of all inner suffering in the renewed creation
Term names the anointing oil as the vehicle of devotion — the costly perfume offered as the expression of inner love and honour toward Christ
Term names the satisfaction of the soul — the inner fullness of delight when the person is nourished by what God provides
Term names the flourishing of the inner person in old age — the fat and fresh quality of the soul that continues to bear fruit through sustained trust in God
Term names the anointing of the Spirit as inner knowledge and protection — the anointing that remains in the person and teaches them from within, guarding against deception
Term names the anointing of the Spirit as inner knowledge and protection — the anointing that remains in the person and teaches them from within, guarding against deception
Term names the Christ as the anointed one whose identity is the object of inner faith — the person of Jesus as the ground of life through belief
Term names union with Christ as the defining inner reality of the person — being in Christ as the location of the transformed inner life

Term names the death and resurrection of Christ as the inner transformative foundation — Christ died for sins; this is received truth on which the inner life rests and on which hope of resurrection stands
Term names the anointing of Jesus with the Holy Spirit as the equipping for inner authority and action — Spirit-reception as the source of inner capacity for ministry
Term names the anointing of the believer by God as the ground of inner certainty — divine anointing as the establishing act that makes the person firm in faith
Term names anointing as an expression of honour, love, and hospitality — the outer act of anointing as the vehicle of inner regard for the person
Term names anointing as the designated means of healing prayer — the outer act accompanying faith and intercession in the community of faith
Inner condition of unresolved division — will suspended between two incompatible orientations, committed to neither
Anxious inner preoccupation with material provision, bodily needs, and future security — orientation away from trust toward self-concerned worry
Inner state of anticipatory anxiety in the face of threat — worry about what to say or do under pressure
Divided or directed inner orientation of care — for the things of God or for worldly matters; also genuine care for others
Inner state of anticipatory pre-worry — anxiety about a future threatening situation before it arrives
Inner orientation of active expectation and watchful waiting — person directed toward what is coming, with hope, longing, or anxiety
Eschatological expectation as governing inner orientation — whole inner life directed toward and shaped by what God has promised
Anxious unsettling thoughts pressing upon the heart — inner person as site of accumulated care and disturbance
Anxious preoccupation with worldly concerns that competes with and displaces inner spiritual receptivity
Inner burden of care for others carried as expression of relational and pastoral orientation
Expectation as an inner state with affective weight — anticipation carrying dread, foreboding, or intensity of directed attention
Inner condition of ease and security — freedom from dread as positive state, often fruit of trust or divine promise
Complacent ease — inner condition of undisturbed settledness that has become spiritual stagnation
Inner condition of freedom from anxious care — the state of being untroubled as a desirable orientation of the inner person
Inner condition of relief from anxiety — reduction of grief-laden concern through wellbeing of a beloved person
Life as contingent on inner-being orientation — obedience, fear, seeking, and choosing God as the condition of life; also surviving divine encounter
Word of God and the Spirit as source of inner-being life — life received from God's word, promise, or Spirit as sustaining reality of the inner person
Divine revival of the inner person — God restoring life to the spirit and heart of those depleted, contrite, or despairing
Inner-being wrestling with life — weight, value, perplexity, and hope of being alive in the face of death
God's gift of reviving — inner-being renewal of a people or person after affliction, understood as divine favour overturning desolation
Living God (Aramaic) as object of inner-being acknowledgment, worship, and trust — enduring reality as ground for inner person's orientation
The living as those with capacity for knowledge of God and intercession — inner-being capacities distinctive to those alive
Living in inner-being suspension — physically alive but existentially and relationally confined, as if dead
Living God as ground and object of the inner person's deepest orientation — soul's thirst, worship, and trust directed to the living God
Life as the God-given condition within which the inner person exists, fears, and responds — fearing God all one's days
Inner-being quality of the living — awareness of mortality, hope, wisdom, and thanksgiving as distinguishing capacities of those alive
Life as fruit of divine redemption and inner-being turning — restoration to life as outcome of repentance, trust, or divine care
God-fearer as inner-being identity — those characterised by reverence for God as their defining mark, recipients of divine attention and blessing
Fear as inner state of anxiety or dread directed at human threats — inner person unsettled by danger, opposition, or uncertain outcomes
Fear of God as foundational inner-being orientation — commanded disposition of the whole person from which obedience, love, and faithfulness flow
God-fearer as inner-being identity — divine favour, belonging, and blessing attached to the person characterised by reverence for God
Awe at God's acts and nature — inner-being response of wonder and reverence to divine power, holiness, and mighty deeds
Fear of God expressed in communal and relational life — reverence shaping conduct toward others and covenantal faithfulness
Inner state of terror or startled fright — inner person overwhelmed and destabilised by sudden or threatening encounters
Act of terrifying — inner-being impact of overwhelming authoritative force, in context of pastoral authority and its appropriate limits
Inner state of intimidation — inner person unsettled by hostile opposition, contrasted with inner steadiness of those whose salvation is secure
Inner state of dismay — inner person overwhelmed, destabilised, and paralysed by threat, shame, or collapse of what was trusted
Commanded absence of dismay — inner steadiness called for in the face of threat, opposition, or cosmic instability
Inner terror induced by divine action — overwhelming of the inner person by God's power or judgment, including awe of covenantal reverence
Reverent awe as fitting inner disposition for worship of God
Act of embittering — causing bitterness in another through harsh treatment, or receiving revelation that brings both sweetness and bitter weight of judgment
Inner quality of bitterness characterising destructive inner dispositions — bitter jealousy as inner state of the heart
Inner quality of bitter weeping — grief of particular intensity saturated with self-recognition, shame, and weight of moral failure
Bitter rebellion — inner act of wilful embittered resistance against God's word after having heard it; hardening against divine revelation
Bitterness as destructive inner state — disposition of hostility, resentment, or hardness that corrupts the person and damages community
Bitterness as spreading inner root — deeply embedded condition that defiles and corrupts beyond the individual
State of being filled with bitterness — inner person pervaded by bitter anguish, here in context of divine affliction that allows no relief
Private interior quality of bitterness — heart's own bitter knowledge of itself, known to the inner person alone and incommunicable to any other
Bitterness as inner state caused by grief — specific pain of a parent whose child has brought shame; inner sorrow carrying quality of bitterness
Bitter toxic quality of suffering and judgment — poison/gall as inner experience of affliction brought before God, or as divine response to inner corruption
Inner condition of being saturated with bitterness — inner person overwhelmed with bitter suffering inflicted by divine judgment
Prophetic boldness — inner audacity to declare what runs counter to expectation or convention, grounded in divine revelation
Inner quality of confident boldness in apostolic communication — willingness to speak directly and without inhibition, grounded in divine grace
Presumptuous boldness — inner disposition of audacious arrogance that displaces fitting reverence, showing no inner trembling before what is holy
Boldness as inner posture of access to God — confident approach to God's presence made possible through Christ, expressed in prayer, worship, and hope of judgment
Boldness as inner quality of apostolic proclamation — confident unrestrained declaration of the gospel grounded in encounter with Christ and sustained through hope
Boldness as inner quality of relational confidence — freedom to speak openly and without self-protective restraint in significant relationships
Bold proclamation as inner-being posture — speaking with unrestrained confidence in the face of opposition and risk, grounded in God
Slave of Christ as inner-being identity — defining self-understanding of the apostle and believer as one wholly given over to Christ's service and authority
Slavery to sin and bondage as former or present inner-being condition apart from God
Faithful servanthood as inner-being quality — stewardship, trustworthiness, and humility before the master as inner dispositions
Social and eschatological levelling of slave and free — inner-being equality of all persons before God transcending social status
Inner-being impossibility of divided ultimate loyalty — heart's singular orientation toward one master; service to God and worldly powers mutually exclusive
Slavery to disordered inner desires — state of the person bound in service to passions, pleasures, and appetite rather than God
Willing service as inner-being expression of love, fervency, and Spirit-led freedom — what distinguishes God-directed service from compelled service
Inner bond of shared servanthood — relational identity of those who serve together under the same Lord, characterised by solidarity and common orientation toward God
Slavery to righteousness and God — inner-being reorientation from bondage to sin to willing service of God, producing holiness
Inner bondage to disordered desires — state of the inner person enslaved to passions, pleasures, or substances
Voluntary servanthood for others — inner act of self-subordination in service of another's flourishing
Inner posture of the Lord's servant — willing humble availability to God's word and purpose as defining inner-being self-identification
Act of bringing others into bondage — external imposition of spiritual or relational enslavement that threatens the inner freedom given by Christ
Inner act of bringing the body under disciplined governance — inner will asserting mastery over bodily impulses in service of spiritual integrity
Spiritual slavery as inner-being condition — bondage to fear, law, or death that characterises the person apart from Christ's liberation
Eschatological liberation from bondage — creation and persons set free from corruption into the freedom of God's glory
Inner experience of being overwhelmed — waves of death, divine wrath, or affliction passing through the inner person, threatening to engulf completely
Inner experience of being at the threshold of birth — extreme distress and exhaustion at the crisis moment, and failure to respond when the moment demands emergence
Breaking of the heart — inner person shattered by grief, expressed in embodied anguish as a sign before others
Breaking of the spirit — inner person shattered by perversion, pride, or affliction, with the spirit explicitly named as the site of breaking
Inner wound — the deep hurt of person or people that demands honest naming and resists superficial healing
Brokenness healed and brokenness mourned — God's binding up of the broken inner person, and inner grief of those who feel the breaking of others

Term names the inner condition of fearful dread — the fears that oppress the person, whether delivered by God or brought upon oneself by wickedness
Term names the quality of divine judgment or presence as terror-inducing — the inner state of dread that the encounter with the holy God produces
Term names terror-producing events or phenomena — the things that generate overwhelming inner fear at the end of the age
Term names the inner condition of fear before a powerful enemy — those whose power generates inner dread in the one who faces them
Term names the inner condition of fearless freedom — serving, speaking, and acting without fear as the positive inner state of liberation through God
Term names the absence of godly fear as an inner moral failure — feasting without reverent restraint as the inner condition of the shameless
Term names the God-fearer as one whose inner orientation of reverence and obedience opens divine attentiveness
Term names the inner state of panic that seizes the person or community, immobilizing composure and overthrowing resolve
Term names reverence as the inner posture of godly fear before God — the disposition that makes prayer heard and worship acceptable
Term names the shuddering horror that seizes the inner being under overwhelming dread, appalling revelation, or imminent catastrophe
Term names the inner state of overwhelming terror before divine presence — the total inner overturn of the person in the face of the holy
Term names terror sent by God as an instrument of divine power — the overwhelming inner dread God places on enemies before Israel
Term names the terror of God experienced by the individual — the inner condition of acute dread before divine presence or judgment
Term names terror as the quality of overwhelming power — the dread that powerful persons, creatures, or situations generate in others
Term names the comprehensive inner terror of being surrounded on every side — the overwhelming existential condition of fear from which there is no escape
Term names the inner spirit of timidity — the fearful, cowardly disposition that God has not given, contrasted with power, love, and self-control
Term describes the inner astonishment, wonder, or stupefaction that overwhelms a person before the extraordinary acts of God or the collapse of expected order
Term names reverential awe before divine power — the inner posture of standing in awe of God, called for from all creatures
Term names inner dread before a threatening power — the practical fear of persons, nations, or forces that can harm
Term describes the inner experience of dread or terror under threat — a fear that destabilizes, paralyzes, or consumes the person inwardly
Term describes the inner orientation of reverential fear and awe toward God — the fear that is the proper and blessed response to divine majesty, word, and goodness
Term describes the absence or displacement of fear — inner peace, confidence, and security that replaces dread through trust in God
Term names the theophanic or miraculous inner fear — the overwhelming inner response to divine presence, miracle, or power, leading to awe and worship
Term names the reverential fear of God as the inner orientation of godly life — fearing God who judges both body and soul; the inner disposition that shapes worship and ethics
Term names the practical or self-interested inner fear before humans or consequences — the inner calculation of social, political, or physical danger that drives action
Term names commanded reverence before God — the inner orientation of trembling fear before the living God, imposed by royal decree
Term names the inner terror produced by visions, powers, or threatening presences — the overwhelming inner dread before what is terrifying or incomprehensible
Term names the inner condition of fearful dread — the apprehensive inner state before what one fears may befall oneself
Term names the inner disposition of fear that is commanded against in the context of divine reassurance and the uniqueness of God

Term names the reverential fear of the Lord as the proper inner orientation — fearing God rather than humans as the inner disposition of covenant loyalty, worship, and moral conduct
Term names the practical inner fear of threatening powers — the inner condition of dread before enemies, authorities, or circumstances that threaten life or wellbeing
Term describes the inner dissolution of heart and spirit under overwhelming fear, dread, or threatening circumstance — the collapse of resolve and courage before a superior force or divine presence
Term names reverential fear that produces faithful obedience — the inner disposition of awe-driven trust that acts on divine warning
Term names practical inner fear for physical safety — the inner assessment of danger that produces protective action
Term names trembling before divine theophanic presence — the total inner agitation produced by the overwhelming encounter with God
Term names trembling in fear before threatening powers or circumstances — the inner agitation of panic and dread
Term names the inner condition of promised security — dwelling without trembling; the eschatological rest from fear as the divine gift
Term describes the inward disposition of dread or terror before a threatening force — a fear that collapses resolve and drives flight or paralysis; the commanded opposite of courage
Term describes the inner orientation of holy reverence and awe before God — the fear that is the proper response to divine majesty and holiness
Term names the inner state of trembling fear — an overwhelming inner agitation produced by the presence of the divine or the terrifying, expressing itself in the body
Term names the inner condition of awe-filled trembling before divine encounter — the overwhelming inner agitation that seizes those at the boundary of the sacred
Term names the inner disposition of trembling reverential fear in godly service — the humble, dependent inner attitude that characterises authentic Christian obedience and ministry
Term names the inner condition of overwhelming trembling before the sacred or the divine — the total inner agitation that seizes those at the boundary of the holy
Term names the inner condition of panic and dread — the acute inner terror before threatening circumstances, battle, or judgement
Term names the inner condition of trembling at the word of God — the responsive, reverent trembling that characterises the person God looks upon with favour
Term names the inner condition of fearful trembling before threatening circumstances or the sacred — the inner agitation of fear or reverent anxiety
Term names the inner-somatic trembling that characterises the human response to the presence and power of God — whether as fearful collapse in the guilty or as the posture of reverential worship
Term names the participant in a shared inner-spiritual reality — one who shares in divine nature, suffering, comfort, or cultic alignment through inner disposition and orientation
Term names the participant in a moral or relational bond — one whose inner orientation places them in solidarity or complicity with another person or cause

Term names the inner-relational orientation of shared life and communion with God — the spiritual participation in the divine nature, the Son, and the Spirit that constitutes the believer's vertical fellowship
Term names the inner-relational bond of mutual belonging, shared commitment, and generous participation among believers — the horizontal fellowship that arises from shared life in Christ

Term describes the loss of distinctive inner character — the becoming flat or flavourless through failure to maintain one's orientation as God's people

Term names the consequences of the fool's inner condition — the ruin and mortality that result from persisting in foolishness
Term names the foolishness of a leader who abandons responsibility — an inner-being disposition of negligence and self-interest over duty

Term names folly as an object of the searching inner mind — a condition placed alongside wisdom as something the person investigates and must understand
Term names foolishness as a disposition of the inner person — a mode of self-presentation and reasoning that departs from the wisdom and orientation of God
Term names folly as the characteristic inner condition of the person who lacks wisdom — an orientation of the heart that produces destructive speech, hasty action, and self-deception
Term names folly as the inner root of emotional volatility and moral failure — hasty temper, self-produced ruin, and the folly confessed before God as sin
Term names the inner condition of foolishness as unpreparedness and failure to think ahead — the disposition that hears but does not act, or acquires without foresight
Term names the inner-epistemic reversal through which God chooses the world's foolish and calls the wise to embrace apparent folly — the paradox of wisdom in Christ
Term names the inner condition of moral/spiritual blindness or contemptuous judgment — the foolishness that fails to discern what is sacred, or that breeds controversy

Term names the abandonment of what is owed — leaving a spouse, neglecting the weightier matters of the law, or forsaking first love — as an inner failure of loyalty and relational commitment

Term names God's act of releasing a person from the guilt and penalty of sin — the vertical forgiveness that restores the inner standing of the person before God
Term names the human act of forgiving another — the horizontal release of an offender from the inner claim of the offended, commanded as the pattern for those who have received divine forgiveness
Term names the act of permitting or granting access as an inner-being posture — the receptivity, welcome, or restraint that reflects an inner orientation of priority, reverence, or obedience

Term names God's act of forgiving sin as the outcome of atonement — the cultic and covenantal restoration of the person's standing before God through sacrifice and priestly mediation
Term names the intercession for divine forgiveness — the act of seeking God's pardon on behalf of oneself or others, and God's response of pardoning or withholding
Term names God's promised and proclaimed forgiveness in the new covenant context — the unconditional covenantal act by which God forgives iniquity and remembers sin no more
Term names forgiveness as a characteristic attribute of God's inner being — the disposition of readiness to pardon that belongs to his essential goodness and steadfast love

Term names forgiveness as a divine attribute and possession — a quality that belongs to God's character, that grounds the possibility of the human-divine relationship, and that evokes reverential fear

Term names the doing of good as the inner-being disposition and practice that characterises those who belong to God — expressed in love for enemies, endurance through unjust suffering, and righteous conduct before others

Term names the person characterised by doing good — a moral-character designation that reflects an inner orientation visible in conduct and recognised by others

Term names the inner conflict between the person's desire for good and their inability to perform it — the good I want but cannot do, the good that exposes the power of sin in the inner person
Term names the experience of being afflicted or oppressed — the inner suffering, loss of dignity, and vulnerability of those under the power of another person or system

Term names fasting as the physical expression of an inner-being posture of grief, penitence, and humble submission before God

Term names human moral character and conduct as good — the inner quality of the person who walks uprightly, acts honestly, and whose character is recognised and assessed as good before God and others

Term names God's good word and promise — the covenantal faithfulness through which God declares, fulfils, and sustains his promised good toward his people

Term names the moral assessment of conduct, ways, or deeds as not good — the prophetic and wisdom verdict that certain inner orientations and behavioural expressions are contrary to the good God requires
Term names goodness as an inner-being disposition and Spirit-produced fruit — a quality that fills the person and is completed by God, expressed in righteous conduct and resolve

Term names the inner-being condition of grievous weakness and wound — the pain that afflicts and weakens the person or community, which God inflicts purposively and heals

Term names the aching pain of the inner being — the heart that aches beneath laughter, the affliction brought to God, the pain God inflicts and heals

Term names the inner groaning and roaring of anguish — the tumult of the heart expressed as an uncontainable inner sound under suffering and threat

Term names the inner groaning under oppression, desolation, and judgment — the anguished inner cry when the outer world collapses

Term names the inward act of perverting and committing iniquity — the moral distortion of the inner being that is confessed, punished, and turned from in repentance
Term names the sinner as the one Jesus came to call — the person whose inner condition of sinfulness is both their social designation and the ground of divine mercy toward them

Term names sin as the offence before God that is confessed, forgiven, and covered — the inner-being condition addressed through repentance, intercession, and divine cleansing

Term names the sinner as a moral-character type whose inner orientation persistently transgresses against God — contrasted with the righteous in the community of God
Term names the punishment that corresponds to sin — the divine consequence that falls on those who persist in sin, which itself becomes a form of chastisement

Term names the moral ground of a person's standing — the charge, reason, or basis for guilt or innocence, including the declared innocence of Christ and the inner conviction that grounds Paul's suffering without shame
Term names the causal ground of guilt or salvation — the found absence of guilt in Christ and his being the source of eternal salvation
Term names the conscience as inner moral witness — it bears testimony, accuses, or excuses the person regarding their conduct before God and others
Term names the conscience as morally vulnerable — susceptible to defilement, weakening through wrong associations, or cauterisation by persistent sin
Term names the conscience as the interior moral foundation of godly living — holding faith and conduct with a clear or good conscience

Term names the inner-being event of incurring and realising guilt — the moment when a person becomes guilty before God and the recognition of that guilt that precedes confession and atonement
Term names the bearing of guilt and its communal consequences — the guilt that brings judgment on individuals and nations who persist in sin, including inner acknowledgement and confession

Term names the guilt offering as the divinely instituted response to guilt — the atonement mechanism that addresses the inner-being condition of guilt, supremely fulfilled in the Servant's soul as the guilt offering
Term names the state of being guilty — the inner recognition of guilt named by the person themselves, including the communal conscience that speaks before God
Term names guiltiness as the accumulated inner-spiritual condition of a person or community before God — the weight of guilt that accumulates, is confessed, and is addressed through atonement
Term names sin as the guilt and offence before God that requires forgiveness — the relational breach that Christ came to address, that is confessed, atoned for, and forgiven through his blood

Term names sin as an indwelling power that rules, enslaves, and deceives the inner being — the force that reigns in the mortal body, dwells within the person, and wages war against the law of the mind
Term names sin as the universal human condition and objective state of all persons apart from Christ — the condition that defines, condemns, and is overcome only by the righteousness of God
Term names the hardening of the inner being — the heart or mind rendered impervious to spiritual perception, understanding, and responsiveness to God
Term names the state of hardening — the condition of the inner being rendered insensible to God, expressed as hardness of heart producing spiritual blindness and alienation
Term names the state of being disfavoured or unloved — an inner relational orientation of lesser love or active disfavour that carries legal and relational consequence
Term names a settled inner hostile disposition — persistent hatred or grudge-bearing directed toward another person, driving external aggression or threatening intent
Term names hatred as an inner hostile disposition directed toward another person — a settled enmity that motivates action, can be concealed, and stands in direct contrast to love
Term names hatred as an attributed inner disposition of God toward a people — projected or perceived divine enmity used to interpret suffering or covenant relationship
Term names hatred as a fundamental inner human capacity — listed alongside love and envy as constitutive orientations of the person that define relational existence and cease at death
Term names hatred as an inner disposition — an established hostile attitude toward God or others that characterises the person's orientation and draws divine judgment
Term names holiness as an inner moral quality of the person — the character of being set apart inwardly from defilement, expressed in conduct and qualifying the person for service and worship

Term names holiness as the inner moral character and set-apart orientation by which the person is consecrated to God — the quality of the person's inner life that corresponds to God's own holiness and is required for approach to him
Term names the person's inner response — reverence, worship, and desire — evoked by encountering the holiness of God in his sanctuary or name
Term names the Spirit's holiness as the divine inner presence that grieves over human rebellion and is placed within the people by God — the inner holy presence as relational reality

Term names sanctification as the inner moral condition and transformative process by which a person is set apart for God — the inward character of holiness as the goal of Christian life and the fruit of the Spirit's work
Term names holiness as the inner moral quality of the person actively pursued and established in the heart — the condition of being set apart inwardly before God
Term names holiness as the inner moral character of the renewed person — the quality of the whole life lived before God, paired with righteousness as constitutive of the new self
Term names holiness as the inner moral character God calls the person to embody — the imperative to be holy as God is holy, grounding the person's inner life in the character of God

Term names the inner orientation of the person toward the Holy One — the response of the inner being (trust, awe, joy, rejection, unwillingness) directed toward God's holiness as a living relational reality
Term names holiness as the identity-constituting set-apart status of the person before God — the person's sacred standing that shapes who they are in relation to God and community
Term names holiness as the divine character quality that the person is disciplined to share — an inner transformation making the person a partaker of God's own holiness
Term names the righteous person's inner moral character — the constitutive orientation of the whole inner being toward what is right before God and others
Term names righteousness as the standard by which God tests and vindicates — the inner moral condition God sees, approves, rewards, and which may be abandoned
Term names the inner response of the righteous person toward God — orientation of trust, joy, and praise
Term names righteousness as the inner moral character of the person — right relationship with God and others expressed in just conduct, faithful dealing, and integrity
Term names righteousness as the status credited to or vindicated in the person by God — moral standing before God, including imputation
Term names righteousness as the divine character quality received, longed for, and responded to — gift, stream of salvation, ground of inner hope and exultation
Term names the inner state of security or settled ease — the condition of feeling safe and undisturbed, even when groundless
Term names the inner act of looking to God — the orientation of the person's gaze toward God in prayer, trust, hope, or longing
Term names looking that discloses an inner state — contempt, envy, mourning, longing, or divine regard withheld — where the looking reveals an inner disposition
Term names the divine act of looking — God's inner attentive regard toward humanity, which is relational inner engagement
Term names the inner act of hoping in God — the orientation of the soul toward God's steadfast love and salvation as the ground of sustained expectation
Term names hope tested or disappointed — waiting in vain, hoping for good only to receive evil, or hoping despite anguish and slaying
Term names the inner act of patient waiting — the posture of sustained expectant attention, whether directed toward God, toward counsel, or through suffering toward renewal
Term names confidence as an inner quality — the settled inner assurance grounded in fear of God and integrity of conduct
Term names the inner condition of cognitive and moral dullness — the stupidity that results from following false instruction and worshipping worthless things
Term names the refuge or shelter sought — the object toward which the inner act of seeking safety is directed, here misplaced and turned to shame
Term names hope as the inner orientation of a people toward God — the expectation of restoration and faithfulness grounded in God as the source of communal hope
Term names confidence in God — the inner quality of assurance and reliance directed toward God as the ground of blessing and security
Term names confidence in inadequate objects — trust placed in gold, human strongholds, or false gods, which is severed or leads to shame
Term names hope as the fundamental inner expectation of the person — the capacity for anticipating good, future, and restoration, present or lost, fulfilled or cut off
Term names expectation as an inner orientation — the forward-looking hope directed at a specific source, here misplaced and turned to shame at its collapse
Term names hope as an inner quality — the expectation of the person that produces joy when fulfilled and heart-sickness when deferred
Term names the perishing or collapse of hope — inner hope withdrawn, lost, or proven false
Term names hope in God — the inner orientation of trust and expectation toward God as the source of blessing and protection
Term names God as the inner person's refuge — the place of safety and strength to which the whole person turns in distress, fear, and trouble
Term names a misplaced inner refuge — false security found in lies or inadequate protection, which God will sweep away
